race.name: L'Etape Brasil by Tour de France
race.display_name: L'Etape Brasil by Tour de France
race.tagline: ASO's Tour de France franchise brings closed-road mountain racing to the Serra da Mantiqueira -- Latin America's biggest amateur cycling event, 10+ years deep.

vitals.location: Campos do Jordao, Sao Paulo State, Brazil (flagship stage; series also visits Cunha and Rio de Janeiro)
vitals.location_badge: CAMPOS DO JORDAO, SAO PAULO, BRAZIL
vitals.field_size: 3,500+ riders (Campos do Jordao); 6,000+ annually across all Brazilian stages
vitals.registration: Online via letapebrasil.com.br or ticketsports.com.br. Entry ~R$500-700 BRL per stage. Combo 'Tripler' discount for all three stages.
vitals.feed_zones: Multiple feed zones with water, energy drinks, fruit, bars. Shimano neutral mechanical support on course.
vitals.cutoff_time: Broom wagon enforced. Riders behind time cutoffs are pulled from course.
route_options: Long Course (Percurso Longo): 107 km, 2,300 m elevation gain
Short Course (Percurso Curto): 66 km, 1,300 m elevation gain

climate.primary: Late Brazilian winter / early spring (September) at mountain altitude
climate.description: September marks the transition from dry winter to spring in the Serra da Mantiqueira. Campos do Jordao sits at 1,600-1,700m elevation, producing notably cooler temperatures than lowland Brazil. Morning starts can be genuinely cold -- the 2025 edition saw 5C wind chill at the 6:00 AM start. Afternoons warm to 18-22C (65-72F). September is among the driest months, with roughly 80mm of rain across the whole month. Humidity is moderate at 60-66%. The combination of cold starts and pleasant afternoon riding conditions is generally favorable for racing.
challenges: Cold morning starts: 5-8C (41-46F) at 6:00 AM launch, with wind chill potentially lower
Temperature swing: 15-17 degree delta from dawn to midday requires layering strategy
Occasional spring storms can develop, though September is statistically dry
Mountain fog possible on higher elevations in early morning

terrain.primary: Paved mountain roads through the Serra da Mantiqueira
terrain.surface: 100% sealed roads through Sao Paulo state countryside. Well-maintained Brazilian mountain highways and rural roads winding through the Serra da Mantiqueira range. Fully closed to traffic on race day. Steep pitches, switchback descents, and rolling terrain between major climbs. Road surface quality is generally good -- inspected and prepared for the event.
features: Fully closed roads -- no traffic, Tour de France race conditions
Serra da Mantiqueira mountain range -- 'Brazilian Switzerland'
Steep climbs with sustained gradients and punchy ramps
Sweeping descents through Atlantic Forest-adjacent terrain
Two distance options: 107 km (long) and 66 km (short)
Tour de France classification jerseys (Yellow, Polka-Dot, Green, White)
Shimano neutral mechanical support on course
Wave starts for controlled field management

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 63. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 63.

course_description.character: Mountain road course through the Serra da Mantiqueira -- Brazil's 'Swiss Alps.' The 107 km long course packs 2,300m of climbing into a compact route that winds through pine forests, mountain ridges, and the scenic countryside surrounding Campos do Jordao. The terrain is relentlessly hilly: there is very little flat riding between the climbs. Gradients alternate between sustained 5-7% drags and punchy 10%+ ramps. The course starts and finishes in the Capivari district, creating a natural amphitheater for the start/finish spectacle.
course_description.signature_challenge: The accumulated climbing density: 2,300m of elevation gain packed into 107 km means you are climbing for approximately 60% of the race. There is no extended flat recovery between climbs. Every descent transitions directly into the next ascent. The compact, relentless nature of the climbing separates this from longer, flatter gran fondos.

logistics.airport: Sao Paulo Guarulhos (GRU) -- international hub, 180 km / 2.5-3 hours drive to Campos do Jordao. Sao Jose dos Campos (SJK) -- domestic, 85 km / 1.5 hours.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Campos do Jordao has abundant accommodation ranging from budget pousadas to upscale mountain resorts. The Capivari district (town center) offers the most options within walking distance of the event village. Book 2-3 months in advance for race weekend as the town fills with riders and supporters. Hotel Toriba is the official event hotel. Airbnb options are plentiful in the mountain chalets surrounding town.
logistics.food: Campos do Jordao is known for fondue, trout, chocolate shops, and European-influenced mountain cuisine. Excellent carb-loading options. Post-race food vendors in the event village. Feed zones on course stocked with water, energy drinks, fruit, bars, and Brazilian snacks.

history.origin_story: L'Etape Brasil launched in October 2015 in Cunha, Sao Paulo state, as part of ASO's global expansion of the L'Etape brand to South America. The inaugural edition attracted strong interest as the first official Tour de France amateur cycling event in Latin America. Eder Amorim won the first edition, completing the 112 km course in 3 hours 31 minutes. The event grew rapidly, expanding from a single Cunha stage to a three-stage Brazilian series: Cunha (March), Rio de Janeiro (June), and Campos do Jordao (September). By 2024, the event celebrated its 10th anniversary and attracted over 6,000 participants annually. Major Brazilian fintech Nubank became presenting sponsor, and the event now represents the pinnacle of amateur road cycling in Latin America.
notable_moments: 2015: Inaugural edition in Cunha, SP. 112 km course. Eder Amorim wins in 3h31m.
Series expands to three annual stages: Cunha, Rio de Janeiro, and Campos do Jordao
Campos do Jordao becomes the flagship stage, hosting 8 consecutive editions through 2025
2022: 3,250 riders at Campos do Jordao; live streaming introduced for the first time
2024: 10th anniversary celebration. 6,000+ annual participants. Nubank as presenting sponsor.
2025: Cunha course reformulated to 110 km with 2,400m elevation. Campos do Jordao draws 3,500 riders.
2026: Three stages confirmed -- Cunha (March 29), Serra Negra (June 27), Campos do Jordao (September 27)
history.reputation: The most prestigious and professionally organized amateur cycling event in Latin America. The Tour de France brand, combined with 10+ years of consistent execution and strong corporate sponsorship, has established L'Etape Brasil as the gold standard for mass-participation cycling in South America. Within Brazilian cycling, completing a L'Etape stage -- especially the mountainous Campos do Jordao edition -- is a significant credential.

biased_opinion.verdict: The Tour de France Goes Tropical (Well, Almost)
biased_opinion.summary: L'Etape Brasil brings ASO's Tour de France franchise to South America with impressive execution -- 107 km of closed-road mountain climbing through the Serra da Mantiqueira, classification jerseys, Shimano support, and 3,500+ riders chasing yellow in the 'Brazilian Switzerland.' Now in its second decade, this is the largest amateur cycling event in Latin America, and it runs with the professional-grade infrastructure you expect from the Tour de France brand. Campos do Jordao delivers 2,300m of genuine climbing in a charming mountain resort town where dawn starts at 5C give way to warm afternoon celebrations. The Brazilian cycling community brings infectious energy that makes the event experience genuinely special. The limitation is scale and context: this is a franchise event, not the original, and the 107 km course is shorter than the French edition's 170 km. The competitive field is strong domestically but thin internationally. What it does brilliantly is bring world-class cycling event production to a growing market that deserves it.
strengths: Fully closed roads with ASO-standard Tour de France production quality
10+ years of consistent operation -- no cancellation problems
2,300m of climbing through the stunning Serra da Mantiqueira mountains
Classification jerseys (Yellow, Polka-Dot, Green, White) create genuine competitive structure
Brazilian cycling community brings warmth and enthusiasm unmatched by more reserved European events
Affordable by L'Etape franchise standards -- ~$100 USD entry fee
Three-stage annual series (Cunha, Rio, Campos do Jordao) enables a season-long engagement
Major corporate sponsors (Nubank, Shimano, Mitsubishi) ensure sustained investment
weaknesses: Regional franchise event -- does not carry the prestige of the French original
107 km course is shorter than most L'Etape editions worldwide
Shallow international competitive field -- overwhelmingly domestic participation
Remote mountain location requires significant ground transfer from Sao Paulo airport
Limited international visibility despite being Latin America's largest amateur cycling event
Cold dawn starts surprise riders expecting tropical Brazilian weather
biased_opinion.bottom_line: L'Etape Brasil is the real deal in franchise terms -- ASO production values, closed roads, serious climbing, and a cycling community that makes every rider feel like they belong. It is the gateway event for Brazilian cycling and the most professionally run mass-participation road event in South America. If you are traveling to Brazil or based in South America, this belongs on your calendar. The Campos do Jordao stage is the crown jewel: 2,300m of mountain climbing, a charming resort town, and Tour de France atmosphere without European pricing. For the pure L'Etape experience, the French original remains supreme. But for the combination of value, community, and the sheer novelty of chasing a yellow jersey through Brazilian mountains, this delivers.

final_verdict.one_liner: Latin America's biggest amateur cycling event: ASO-organized, closed-road mountain racing through Brazil's Serra da Mantiqueira with Tour de France classification jerseys and 3,500 riders.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want Tour de France atmosphere at a fraction of European pricing. Yes if you are based in South America and want the most professionally organized sportive on the continent. Yes if 2,300m of mountain climbing on closed roads through stunning scenery sounds like your kind of weekend. Skip if you want the prestige of the French original -- this is a franchise, not the main event. Skip if you need deep international competition -- the field is overwhelmingly domestic.
final_verdict.alternatives: For the definitive L'Etape experience: L'Etape du Tour de France (France, July). For L'Etape in the Southern Hemisphere: L'Etape Australia (NSW, November). For a road gran fondo in the Americas with deeper competitive fields: GFNY New York or GFNY Miami.

In 2018 L'Etape Brasil by Le Tour de France took place around Campos do Jord√£o. Hills, passes and amazing landscapes, relive this edition watching the most beautiful images. 

---------

L'Etape by Le Tour de France is a series of amateur cycling events designed to experience Le Tour de France Legend. It is the only event where you can race and feel like a Tour de France champion, and where you can enjoy the Tour de France unique atmosphere and passion.

‚ÄúThe Tour de France is the world's greatest cycling race. Each year it is passionately followed by fans around the world so it has naturally given rise to L'Etape by Le Tour de France series‚ÄĚ, said Christian Prudhomme, Tour de France director. The series has now expanded to 14 international locations including Colombia, Wales, Mexico, China, and Australia.

Among these Tour de France official cycling events is of course L'√Čtape du Tour. With a peloton of 15 000 riders and 50 nationalities, it is Europe's biggest cycling event. Riding on the same routes and under the same conditions as Tour de France champions: this is the promise that L'√Čtape du Tour has kept since 1993.

rider_intel.search_text: The available transcripts for L'Etape Brasil by Tour de France consist entirely of promotional music videos with lyrics rather than rider commentary or race analysis. The videos emphasize themes of heat, mountains, and challenging terrain through song lyrics that mention racing the road ahead, mountains bowing to burning sun, chasing shadows, each breath being fire, asphalt whispering warnings, and summits calling through the air. While the lyrics suggest a challenging mountain course with significant heat factors and elevation changes, no specific course details, rider experiences, gear recommendations, or actionable race intelligence is provided in these promotional materials.
